scope: Features computed by aggregating data over specific groups or categories. This approach captures context beyond a single row, such as the behavior of an entity relative to its peers.
key_concepts: Aggregations group data by a categorical feature and calculate statistical summaries (mean, median, max, min, count, standard deviation) for numerical features.
Group-based features provide contextual information, allowing a model to understand a single observation in relation to its broader group.
These features are particularly powerful when the target behavior is driven by relative deviations rather than absolute values.
Aggregations can be merged back into the original dataset to enrich the row-level observations.

definitions.definition: A feature created by calculating an aggregate statistic for a specific category and assigning that value to all members of the category.

examples.description: Calculating the "average transaction amount per user" and joining it back to the individual transaction rows.
Creating a "relative price" feature by subtracting the "average house price in the neighborhood" from the individual "house price".

relations.builds_toward: Relational and event-log features in ch06_s04, which extends this to temporal and multi-table aggregations.
relations.prerequisites: Basic understanding of data frames and groupings.
